required.title: CS Unplugged — Activities for Young Learners
A set of simple logic puzzles

suggested.title: Swirl by Swirl: Spirals in Nature
suggested.author: Joyce Sidman

suggested.title: If You Give a Mouse a Cookie
suggested.author: Laura Numeroff

supplementary.title: Sorting and classifying collections from nature

competence_markers.description: Can arrange a set of steps into a correct sequence and explain why the order matters
Can identify a pattern in objects, sounds, or events and state the rule that governs it
Can create a pattern for someone else to decode and continue
Can write or dictate a procedure precise enough for another person to follow without additional guidance
Can articulate at least five "if-then" rules from daily life
Can demonstrate the difference between random and systematic search
Can sort the same set of objects by different rules and explain how the sorting rule changes the outcome
Can explain in their own words what an algorithm is, with a real-life example
Can narrate a process (sorting, searching, following a procedure) clearly and in correct order
Demonstrates the habit of looking for sequences, patterns, and procedures in everyday life

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time today when you followed a sequence. What were the steps? Did the order matter?
Describe a pattern you noticed this week. What is the rule that makes it a pattern? What would come next?
Explain a procedure — any procedure — precisely enough that I could follow it without asking you any questions.
What is an "if-then" rule you follow every day without thinking about it? State it as clearly as you can.
Tell me about the difference between searching randomly and searching systematically. Which do you prefer? Why?
You sorted a group of objects by one rule, then by another. Tell me what changed and what stayed the same.
What is an algorithm? Explain it in your own words, and give me an example from your real life.

cross_connections.description: — Number patterns, counting sequences, and sorting by quantity connect directly to computational pattern recognition.
— Following a procedure carefully and observing what happens is how both algorithms and experiments work.
— "If-then" thinking is the foundation of formal logic; you are building that foundation here.
— Following instructions to build something physical is procedure execution; troubleshooting when it goes wrong is debugging.
— Precise narration of a procedure is a communication skill; vagueness is the enemy of both good writing and good algorithms.
— Even at this age, understanding that a procedure determines an outcome connects to understanding how processes generate results.

content.why_this_matters:
You already think in sequences, patterns, and procedures every single day. When you get dressed in the morning, you follow a sequence — underwear before pants, socks before shoes. When you notice that every other fence post is painted white, you have found a pattern. When you explain to someone exactly how to make your favorite sandwich, step by step, you have written a procedure. This packet is about becoming aware of what your mind already does and learning to do it with intention.

Computational thinking is not about computers. A famous computer scientist named Edsger Dijkstra once said that "computer science is no more about computers than astronomy is about telescopes." What he meant is that the real skill is in the thinking — in learning to see the world as sequences that unfold in order, patterns that repeat and predict, and procedures that can be followed precisely to get a desired result. These are the thinking tools that make everything from cooking to building to exploring nature work better.

In this packet, you will sort, sequence, play pattern games, build treasure hunts, and practice "if-then" thinking — all without ever touching a screen. You will use your body, your hands, real objects, and your own words. By the end, you will see sequences, patterns, and procedures everywhere, because they are everywhere. You just need to learn to notice.
content.what_this_develops:
1. The ability to identify and articulate a sequence of steps in the correct order
2. Pattern recognition — seeing what repeats, what comes next, and why
3. The capacity to formalize a procedure precisely enough that someone else can follow it
4. Conditional reasoning at a concrete level ("if this, then that")
5. Systematic vs. random search strategies — understanding that how you look for something matters
6. The habit of decomposition — breaking a task into smaller, ordered parts
7. Precise verbal narration of processes and reasoning
8. The foundational insight that thinking itself has structure
content.core_content:
### What Is a Sequence?

A sequence is a set of things arranged in a particular order where the order matters. "Put on your shoes, then put on your socks" is a sequence — but it is the wrong one. "Put on your socks, then put on your shoes" is the right sequence. The items are the same. Only the order changed. But the order is everything.

Practice seeing sequences in your daily life. Getting ready for bed is a sequence. Making a peanut butter sandwich is a sequence. Planting a seed is a sequence. The question to always ask is: *Does the order matter? What happens if I change it?*

**Activity — Sequence Cards:** Write or draw each step of a familiar task on separate cards. Scramble them. Put them back in order. Now try giving your scrambled cards to someone else — can they put them in order without being told? If not, you may need more cards (more detail in your steps).

### What Is a Pattern?

A pattern is something that repeats in a predictable way. Red, blue, red, blue, red, blue — you know the next one is red. But patterns can be more complex. Red, red, blue, red, red, blue — now the unit that repeats is three, not two. And patterns are not just colors. They can be sounds (clap, clap, stomp), movements (step, step, jump), numbers (2, 4, 6, 8), or shapes.

The real power of pattern recognition is prediction. When you see a pattern, you can say what comes next before it arrives. This is one of the most important thinking skills a human being can develop.

**Activity — Pattern Creators:** Make a pattern using objects — buttons, blocks, stones, anything. Make it at least three full repetitions long. Now ask someone else to continue it. Then try the reverse: ask someone to make a pattern and you decode it. The challenge is not just seeing the pattern but *stating the rule* — "the rule is: two reds, then one blue, repeating."

### What Is a Procedure?

A procedure is a sequence of steps written precisely enough that anyone can follow them and get the same result. A recipe is a procedure. Assembly instructions are a procedure. When you explain to someone exactly how to walk from your front door to the nearest tree, that is a procedure.

The key word is *precisely*. "Make a sandwich" is not a procedure — it is a wish. "Take two slices of bread, place them side by side on a plate, spread peanut butter on one slice using a knife..." — now you are writing a procedure.

**Activity — The Precise Instruction Game:** One person closes their eyes (or turns away). The other person must give verbal instructions to build something specific with blocks or draw a specific shape — without the listener being able to see the goal. Every vague instruction leads to errors. You will discover quickly how precise language must be for a procedure to work.

### Sorting: The Procedure Determines the Outcome

Take a handful of mixed objects — buttons of different sizes and colors, or a collection of leaves, or a pile of mixed coins. Now sort them. But here is the important thing: *by what rule?* Sort by color and you get one arrangement. Sort by size and you get a completely different one. Sort by weight, different again.

The objects did not change. Only your sorting rule — your *algorithm* — changed. The procedure determines the outcome. This is one of the most important ideas in computational thinking: the same data, processed by different algorithms, gives different results.

**Activity — Sort Three Ways:** Gather 15–20 small objects with varied properties. Sort them one way and describe your rule. Sort them a different way with a different rule. Sort them a third way. Narrate: "When I sorted by color, the groups were... When I sorted by size, the groups were... The objects were the same every time, but the rule I chose changed everything."

### If-Then Thinking

You already live with "if-then" rules every day. If it is raining, then you wear a raincoat. If the timer beeps, then the cookies are done. If you are tired, then you rest. These are *conditional* statements — one thing depends on another.

The skill here is making these rules explicit. Instead of just doing them automatically, you practice *stating* them clearly. "If it is below freezing, then water becomes ice." "If I drop an egg, then it breaks." "If I am kind, then people feel welcome."

**Activity — If-Then Scavenger Hunt:** Walk through your home or yard and identify as many "if-then" rules as you can. Write them down or state them aloud. Some will be physical (if you push a ball, then it rolls), some will be social (if you say thank you, then people feel appreciated), some will be natural (if the sun sets, then it gets dark). Try to find at least ten.

### Search: Random vs. Systematic

You have lost a toy somewhere in your room. You could look randomly — check under the bed, then in the closet, then under the bed again, then in a drawer you already checked. Or you could search systematically — start at one corner, check every spot moving across the room, never checking the same place twice.

Which strategy finds the toy faster? The systematic one, almost every time. This is the beginning of understanding *search algorithms* — the idea that how you look for something matters just as much as whether you look.

**Activity — Treasure Hunt by Strategy:** Hide a small object. First, have someone search randomly (just looking wherever occurs to them). Count how many places they check. Then re-hide it, and have them search systematically (left to right across the room, top to bottom, or room by room). Count again. Repeat several times and compare. Which strategy is more reliable?

### Algorithms Are Everywhere

An algorithm is simply a procedure that solves a problem. Every recipe is an algorithm for making a dish. Every set of directions is an algorithm for reaching a destination. Every morning routine is an algorithm for getting ready.

You are not learning something new and strange. You are learning to see and name something that has been part of your life since the day you were born. The power comes from the naming — from the awareness — because once you can see an algorithm, you can improve it, share it, and debug it when it goes wrong.
content.formative_practices:
1. **The Sandwich Study:** Write a procedure for making a sandwich that is so precise a visitor from another planet could follow it. Test it by having someone follow your instructions *exactly as written* — no guessing, no filling in gaps. What did you forget? What was unclear? Revise and test again.

2. **The Sorting Study:** Collect 20 natural objects from outdoors (leaves, stones, sticks, seeds). Sort them three different ways using three different rules. Narrate what you notice: Do certain objects end up together no matter what rule you use? Do some objects resist easy categorization?

3. **The Pattern Wall:** Over one week, document every pattern you notice — in nature, in your home, in music, in daily routines. Draw or describe each one. At the end of the week, look at your collection: can you sort your patterns into categories?

4. **The Lost Object Experiment:** With a partner, conduct the random vs. systematic search experiment five times. Record the number of checks each time. Narrate your findings. Is systematic always faster? When might random search work just as well?

5. **The If-Then Chain:** Start with one "if-then" statement and build a chain. "If it rains, then the ground is wet. If the ground is wet, then my shoes get muddy. If my shoes get muddy, then I must clean them before coming inside." How long can you make the chain? What do you notice about how one condition leads to the next?

6. **The Morning Algorithm:** Write out your morning routine as a precise procedure. Every step, in exact order. Now look for steps that could be re-ordered — does it matter if you brush your teeth before or after getting dressed? Which steps *must* come in a fixed order? This is the beginning of understanding *dependencies*.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### Sequencing

**Developing well:** The child can take a multi-step task and arrange the steps in correct order, explaining *why* each step must come where it does. They spontaneously notice when steps are out of order in daily life. They can create sequence instructions for others.

**Developing slowly:** The child can follow a sequence but struggles to articulate why the order matters. They may not notice when steps are misordered unless the result is dramatically wrong.

**Diagnostic questions:** "If I put my shoes on before my socks, what happens?" "Can I frost a cake before I bake it? Why not?" "What is the first thing you must do when [familiar task]?"

### Pattern Recognition

**Developing well:** The child identifies patterns quickly, can state the rule governing the pattern, and can generate patterns for others to decode. They notice patterns in their environment unprompted — tile arrangements, rhythms in music, cycles in nature.

**Developing slowly:** The child can continue a simple pattern (ABABAB) but struggles with more complex units (AABAABAAB). They may not spontaneously notice patterns outside of structured activities.

**Diagnostic questions:** "What comes next? How do you know?" "Can you make a pattern that would be hard for me to figure out?" "What is the *rule* behind this pattern?"

### Procedural Precision

**Developing well:** The child can give instructions precise enough for another person to follow without clarification. They naturally include details about quantity, direction, and sequence. When their instructions fail, they can identify the gap and revise.

**Developing slowly:** The child gives vague instructions and becomes frustrated when the follower "does it wrong." They may not yet see that the problem is in the instruction, not in the follower.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Can you tell me how to do that so precisely that I could do it with my eyes closed?" "What did you leave out of your instructions?" "If a stranger followed your directions, where would they get confused?"

### Conditional Thinking

**Developing well:** The child can state "if-then" rules clearly and understands that the "if" is a condition that must be true for the "then" to follow. They can chain conditions together.

**Developing slowly:** The child understands "if-then" in familiar contexts but cannot formulate new conditional statements independently.

**Diagnostic questions:** "What is the rule? Start with 'if...'" "What happens if the 'if' part is NOT true?" "Can you give me an if-then rule about [unfamiliar topic]?"
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Introducing Screens Too Early

The entire point of Stretching Branches is that computational thinking does not require technology. If you introduce a tablet app for "coding for kids," you have replaced deep, embodied, narration-rich learning with a gamified simulation. Resist this completely. Screens come later, in Weathering Storms, and only after the foundational thinking is solid.

### Over-Explaining Instead of Letting the Child Discover

If you explain what a pattern is for fifteen minutes, you have delivered a lecture. If you lay out colored blocks in a pattern and ask "what comes next?", you have created a discovery. The child needs to encounter the concept through experience, then name it through narration. Your explanations should be brief — a sentence or two to name what the child has already experienced.

### Accepting Vague Narration

When the child says "we did the thing with the blocks," do not nod and move on. Gently ask for more. "Which blocks? What did you do with them? In what order? What did you notice?" Thin narration means thin understanding. The fix is always more experience (not more lecturing) followed by another chance to narrate.

### Rushing to Complexity

A child who cannot reliably continue an ABAB pattern is not ready for AABCAABC. A child who cannot sequence a three-step procedure is not ready for seven steps. Build slowly. Mastery at simple levels creates the foundation for complexity. There is no timeline. Stay at each level until narration demonstrates confident understanding.

### Making It Feel Like School

These activities should feel like play — because they are play. Sorting rocks from a nature walk, giving silly instructions to build a block tower, hunting for treasure in the backyard. The moment it feels like a worksheet exercise, you have lost the engagement that makes the learning real. Charlotte Mason's principle of short lessons and living engagement applies with full force here.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: "Tell me about a time today when you followed a sequence."
**Strong:** "When I made my bed, first I pulled the sheet up, then I put the blanket on top, then the pillow. It has to go in that order because the blanket covers the sheet and the pillow goes on top of everything."
**Weak:** "I did stuff in order." (Press for specifics: which stuff? which order? why that order?)

### Prompt 2: "Describe a pattern you noticed this week."
**Strong:** "The bricks on the front of the house go red, red, tan, red, red, tan. The rule is two reds then one tan and it keeps going. I checked and it goes all the way across."
**Weak:** "There were patterns on the wall." (Ask: what kind? what was the rule? could you continue it?)

### Prompt 3: "Explain a procedure precisely enough that I could follow it."
**Strong:** "To sharpen a pencil, hold the pencil in your left hand and the sharpener in your right. Put the point of the pencil into the hole. Turn the pencil clockwise — that means the way a clock goes. Turn it about ten times. Pull it out and check if it is sharp. If not, put it back in and turn five more times."
**Weak:** "You just put it in and turn it." (Ask: put what in what? turn which way? how many times? how do you know when to stop?)

### Prompt 4: "What is an if-then rule you follow every day?"
**Strong:** "If my hands are dirty, then I wash them before eating. The 'if' part is the condition — my hands being dirty. The 'then' part is what I do about it."
**Weak:** "If I am hungry I eat." (This is correct but shallow. Press: can you think of one where the 'if' part is less obvious? Can you make a chain — if A then B, if B then C?)

### Prompt 5: "Tell me about random vs. systematic search."
**Strong:** "When I looked for my toy randomly, I kept checking the same places and missed other places. When I went corner to corner, I found it faster because I did not skip anywhere or repeat anywhere."
**Weak:** "The second way was better." (Ask: why? what made it better? what was different about how you searched?)

### Prompt 6: "You sorted objects by different rules. What changed?"
**Strong:** "When I sorted the buttons by color, all the blues were together and all the reds were together. When I sorted by size, the big blue button was with the big red button instead. The buttons did not change but the groups changed because my rule changed."
**Weak:** "They went into different groups." (Ask: why different groups? what determined which group something went into?)

### Prompt 7: "What is an algorithm?"
**Strong:** "An algorithm is steps you follow to solve a problem or do a task. Like a recipe is an algorithm for making food. My morning routine is an algorithm for getting ready. The steps have to be in order and clear enough that anyone could follow them."
**Weak:** "It is something computers do." (Redirect: can you think of an algorithm that has nothing to do with a computer?)
